17.display: More hours of revision are linked with higher test scores

18.display: Lower temperatures are linked with higher hot chocolate sales

22.display: No — outliers should usually be ignored when drawing the line, since they do not fit the general trend

23.display: Estimating a value between two known data points, within the range of data collected

24.display: Estimating a value beyond the range of data collected, which is less reliable

25.display: Strong correlation means the points lie very close to the line of best fit, with little scatter around it.

26.display: False — correlation shows a relationship between two variables, but it does not prove that one causes the other to change.

27.display: Not true — extrapolation assumes the same pattern continues beyond the data collected, but the relationship between the variables could change outside that range, making the estimate less reliable.

28.display: Never — if there is no clear correlation, drawing a line of best fit would not represent a meaningful trend in the data.

29.display: Flawed because correlation does not mean causation; a third factor, such as hot weather, is likely causing both more ice cream sales and more people swimming (and therefore more drownings).

30.display: An outlier can distort the line of best fit if included, so it is usually ignored when drawing the line, but it might represent a real, valid (if unusual) result, so it should not be deleted from the data without good reason.

31.display: Not necessary — a line of best fit is meant to show the overall trend of all the points, so it usually passes between most points rather than exactly through any of them.

32.display: Positive correlation: as one variable increases, so does the other (e.g. height and shoe size); negative correlation: as one increases, the other decreases (e.g. car age and value); no correlation: no clear relationship (e.g. shoe size and test score).

33.display: Gradient = -2 — the car's value decreases by about £2000 for every extra year of age.

36.display: Driving at a lower speed (below the threshold) improves fuel efficiency, so drivers wanting to save fuel should slow down.

37.display: 45°C is far outside the range of temperatures observed (10–30°C), so the trend might not continue in the same way at such an extreme temperature, making the estimate unreliable.

38.display: Treat it as an outlier — ignore it when drawing the line of best fit and describing the correlation as strong positive, but mention it separately as an unusual case.

40.display: 30; this is interpolation (x = 6 is within the data range 1 to 9)

41.display: Example: the number of firefighters at a fire and the amount of damage caused both increase because of the size of the fire (a third factor), not because more firefighters cause more damage.

42.display: Your own two positively correlated variables and two negatively correlated variables, with reasoning for each.
